title: Contradictions in the Bible
subtitle: Commonly cited narrative tensions, why they matter, and the main responses
summary: This draft examines commonly cited Bible contradictions without confusing them with textual variants or mere differences of emphasis. Each example identifies the competing claims, explains why critics call them contradictory, and notes the principal harmonizing and historical-critical responses.

sections.title: What This Page Does—and Does Not—Call a Contradiction
sections.body:
A contradiction is not simply two passages that sound different. It is a case in which two claims cannot both be true in the same sense and at the same time. This matters because some items in popular lists are real textual variants: manuscripts preserve different wording. Others are compressed accounts that may be combined. Still others remain difficult even after a proposed harmonization.

This page covers narrative, chronological, and numerical tensions. It does not treat later manuscript additions or omissions as contradictions; those belong under textual variants because the question there is which wording is earliest, not whether two assertions can both be true.

sections.title: Creation Sequence and the Creation of Woman
sections.body:
Genesis 1 states that God created humanity as male and female together. Genesis 2 narrates the man, then the woman made from his side after the garden and the animals are discussed. Critics call this a contradiction because, read as two complete chronological accounts, the order of the sexes and the place of the animals in the sequence differ.

A common Jewish and Christian response is that Genesis 2 is not trying to repeat a second day-by-day creation schedule. On that reading it zooms in on the human pair and their vocation, and its reference to animals need not mean every animal was first created after the man. Historical-critical readers often see the chapters as distinct traditions placed together. The tension is real at the level of a flat chronology; whether it is a formal contradiction depends on the genre and sequencing one attributes to Genesis 2.

sections.title: Animals on Noah's Ark
sections.body:
One instruction tells Noah to bring two of every kind of living creature into the ark. A later instruction tells him to take seven pairs of clean animals and birds, while taking two of animals that are not clean. Critics call this contradictory when the first instruction is read as an exhaustive total for every animal and the second as a different exhaustive total.

The standard harmonization is straightforward: two of every kind is the general minimum, while clean animals and birds receive additional pairs. A critical reader may still ask why the instructions are repeated with a changed level of detail, but this example is usually better classified as a harmonizable tension than as a strict contradiction.

sections.title: Who Incited David's Census?
sections.body:
The parallel census stories name different agents. Samuel says that the LORD moved David to count Israel and Judah; Chronicles says that Satan moved David to take the census. Critics call this a contradiction because the same initiating act is assigned in two short, otherwise parallel narratives to two different named agents.

Christian harmonizations commonly distinguish ultimate sovereignty from immediate agency: God permits or judicially gives David over to an act that Satan directly prompts. Historical-critical interpreters instead note that Chronicles often rewrites Samuel-Kings and may replace direct divine causation with Satan as its theology develops. The first approach can reconcile the two claims only if that layered causation is accepted; the second treats the difference as evidence of differing authorial perspectives rather than a single unchanged report.

sections.title: Jesus' Genealogies
sections.body:
Matthew identifies Joseph as the son of Jacob and traces Jesus' Davidic line through Solomon. Luke identifies Joseph as the son of Heli and traces the line through Nathan. The genealogies also diverge at many names between David and Joseph. Critics therefore say the lists cannot both be one simple biological paternal genealogy.

The best-known harmonization says Matthew gives Joseph's legal royal line and Luke gives Mary's biological line, with Heli treated as Joseph's father-in-law. Other proposals appeal to levirate marriage or adoption. None of those explanations is stated explicitly in the two genealogies, which is why critical scholars usually regard the lists as serving different theological and literary purposes. The linked study gives the texts and arguments in more detail.

sections.title: Judas's Death and the Field of Blood
sections.body:
Matthew says Judas left, hanged himself, and the chief priests bought the potter's field with the returned silver. Acts says Judas obtained a field with the reward of his wickedness and fell headlong, bursting open. Critics see two different accounts of both the manner of death and the purchase.

A familiar harmonization says Judas hanged himself first, then his body later fell and burst; it also says the priests bought the field with Judas's money, so Acts can attribute the purchase to him in a practical sense. That scenario is possible, but neither passage narrates the full combined sequence. Historical-critical readings instead treat the two texts as distinct traditions about Judas. The issue should be described as a genuine narrative tension, not dismissed by simply repeating one account.

sections.title: Passover and the Time of Crucifixion
sections.body:
Mark introduces the Last Supper on the day when the Passover sacrifice was made, and its meal is normally read as Passover. John says that the authorities avoid ritual defilement so that they may still eat the Passover, and calls the later trial the preparation day of the Passover. Mark also places the crucifixion at the third hour, while John's trial scene is around the sixth hour.

Critics see an incompatible timeline: on the most direct reading, the Synoptics place the meal at Passover and John places the trial before it. Christian proposals include different calendars, broader uses of the word Passover, and different methods of counting daylight hours. These are substantive proposals rather than a simple textual correction, and readers should examine whether they fit each narrative naturally.

sections.title: Resurrection Reports
sections.body:
The four Gospels name different women at the tomb. Matthew names Mary Magdalene and the other Mary; Mark adds Salome; Luke includes Joanna, Mary the mother of James, and other women; John initially names Mary Magdalene alone. They also vary in the reported time of arrival, the description and number of heavenly messengers, and the sequence of appearances.

A contradiction follows only if each account claims to provide a complete list and a complete timeline. Harmonizers argue that one writer may name only the most prominent person while another gives a larger group, and that several visits or appearances can be arranged in sequence. Critics answer that the omissions and ordering remain substantial and that no Gospel provides the combined chronology. The honest classification is a cluster of narrative tensions whose force depends on whether the accounts are expected to agree in every detail.

sections.title: A Fair Conclusion
sections.body:
These examples should not be used as a substitute for reading whole books or for understanding how Christian communities interpret their scriptures. Nor should they be hidden. Some are readily harmonized if the passages are read as selective or layered; others continue to look like competing reports, especially when numerical details or a single named agent changes.

The right question is not whether a reader can imagine any possible reconciliation. It is whether the proposed reconciliation is stated by the texts, fits their normal language and context, and explains the differences better than the alternative that the writers preserve different traditions or theological emphases.

bibleVerses.text: God created man in his own image. In God’s image he created him; male and female he created them.

Yahweh God caused the man to fall into a deep sleep. As the man slept, he took one of his ribs, and closed up the flesh in its place.
Yahweh God made a woman from the rib which he had taken from the man, and brought her to the man.

Of every living thing of all flesh, you shall bring two of every sort into the ship, to keep them alive with you. They shall be male and female.
Of the birds after their kind, of the livestock after their kind, of every creeping thing of the ground after its kind, two of every sort will come to you, to keep them alive.

You shall take seven pairs of every clean animal with you, the male and his female. Of the animals that are not clean, take two, the male and his female.
Also of the birds of the sky, seven and seven, male and female, to keep seed alive on the surface of all the earth.
Again Yahweh’s anger burned against Israel, and he moved David against them, saying, “Go, count Israel and Judah.”
Satan stood up against Israel, and moved David to take a census of Israel.
Jacob became the father of Joseph, the husband of Mary, from whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ.
Jesus himself, when he began to teach, was about thirty years old, being the son (as was supposed) of Joseph, the son of Heli,

Then Judas, who betrayed him, when he saw that Jesus was condemned, felt remorse, and brought back the thirty pieces of silver to the chief priests and elders, saying, “I have sinned in that I betrayed innocent blood.” But they said, “What is that to us? You see to it.” He threw down the pieces of silver in the sanctuary, and departed. He went away and hanged himself.

Now this man obtained a field with the reward for his wickedness, and falling headlong, his body burst open, and all his intestines gushed out. It became known to everyone who lived in Jerusalem that in their language that field was called ‘Akeldama,’ that is, ‘The field of blood.’
On the first day of unleavened bread, when they sacrificed the Passover, his disciples asked him, “Where do you want us to go and prepare that you may eat the Passover?”
They led Jesus therefore from Caiaphas into the Praetorium. It was early, and they themselves didn’t enter into the Praetorium, that they might not be defiled, but might eat the Passover.
Now it was the Preparation Day of the Passover, at about the sixth hour. He said to the Jews, “Behold, your King!”
It was the third hour, and they crucified him.
Now after the Sabbath, as it began to dawn on the first day of the week, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary came to see the tomb.
When the Sabbath was past, Mary Magdalene, and Mary the mother of James, and Salome, bought spices, that they might come and anoint him.
Now they were Mary Magdalene, Joanna, and Mary the mother of James. The other women with them told these things to the apostles.
Now on the first day of the week, Mary Magdalene went early, while it was still dark, to the tomb, and saw the stone taken away from the tomb.

furtherReading.title: Jesus, Interrupted: Revealing the Hidden Contradictions in the Bible
furtherReading.author: Bart D. Ehrman
furtherReading.note: A readable historical-critical presentation of major tensions in the Bible; readers should also consult scholarly responses and primary texts.

furtherReading.title: The Historical Reliability of the Gospels
furtherReading.author: Craig L. Blomberg
furtherReading.note: An evangelical defense of Gospel reliability that presents harmonizations of alleged discrepancies.

furtherReading.title: The Bible Unearthed
furtherReading.author: Israel Finkelstein and Neil Asher Silberman
furtherReading.note: A widely discussed archaeological and historical-critical study of biblical narratives; its conclusions remain debated among scholars.
